The question asks whether reserve balances will increase between the data released for the week of March 25, 2026, and April 1, 2026. This period corresponds to the turn of the month. Generally, on the first day of the month, there are large, scheduled outflows from the Treasury General Account (TGA) for Social Security, Medicare, and military/retirement pay. When the Treasury makes these payments, funds shift from the TGA to the banking system, which reliably pushes up banks' reserve balances at the Federal Reserve. We can observe this 'first-of-month' effect in recent FRED data: reserves increased from the last week of December to the first week of January, from late Jan to early Feb, and from late Feb to early March. While end-of-quarter Treasury settlements on March 31 could offset some of this increase, the first-of-month payment flows are highly determinative. Consequently, an increase in reserve balances is more likely than not. Elevating from a 50% baseline to 65%.

The Federal Reserve ceased shrinking its aggregate balance sheet in December 2025, but explicitly noted that MBS runoff would continue (with proceeds reinvested into Treasuries). Thus, the change between March 25 and April 1 is almost certainly exactly zero. Because the question asks if the value will have *increased*, a flat value resolves to NO. Given the structural runoff policy and the weekly data reporting pattern, an increase is highly improbable. I limit my estimate to the 0.05 floor for certainty bounds.

Thus, a probability of 0.22 fairly represents the likelihood of Fidesz retaining the most seats, incorporating the tail risk of hybrid-regime structural advantages overriding the popular vote while remaining deferential to the overwhelming polling data favoring Tisza.

The Hungarian electoral system (Mixed-Member Majoritarian) is engineered to magnify plurality wins into 2/3 supermajorities via winner-take-all single-member districts and 'winner compensation' national lists. This produced Fidesz supermajorities in the last four elections (2010-2022). However, the 2026 election features a fundamentally different landscape: the emergence of the unified TISZA party. Rather than Fidesz securing 50% vs a fragmented opposition splitting the remaining vote (enabling Fidesz to easily sweep districts), Fidesz now faces head-to-head district races where the opponent consolidates 40%+ of the vote. 

Recent March 2026 polling has the race remarkably close. Some polls show a heavy TISZA lead (e.g., 38% to 30%, which models project to yield only ~115 seats due to geographic concentration in cities), while Fidesz-aligned pollsters show Fidesz leading (e.g., 46% to 40%, projecting ~117 seats). Because TISZA's base is highly packed in urban centers, TISZA would likely need a massive +15 point national wave to reach 133 seats. Conversely, because Fidesz's support is efficiently distributed across 90 rural districts (and aided by December 2024 redistricting), Fidesz could hit 133 seats with roughly a +10 point national lead, or slightly less if they form a post-election coalition with Mi Hazank.

Currently, polls suggest a narrow single-digit outcome, meaning a supermajority is mathematically unlikely as both parties are projected to land in the 100-125 seat range. A YES resolution requires either a volatile late-stage blowout (a TISZA landslide or a Fidesz resurgence >10%) or a right-wing post-election coalition. While these 'fat tail' scenarios are plausible, the baseline expectation of a closely contested two-party race heavily degrades the structural 'winner's bonus' that previously guaranteed Fidesz 133 seats. The community's 0.66 estimate severely underprices how effectively a unified 40% opposition blocks majoritarian sweeps, so I forecast lower at 0.41.
